text: This video demonstrates the technology and technical capacity of the Airsealer IFS made by SurgeEquest. This is an innovative access and suflation system that continually monitors and adjusts interoperative carbon dioxide flow rates to maintain a fixed volume pneumoperitoneum. It can immediately and automatically compensate for the pressure and volume variability caused by gas loss due to leakage or suctioning, thereby mitigating against diminution of pneumoperitoneum as a consequence.

The patient is positioned in lithotomy for the laparoscopic loop defunctioning sigmoid colostomy. The air seal trocar and tubing system are visibly positioned subumbilically using the HESAM technique.

The surgeon excises a trephine at the preoperatively marked site for stoma formation in the left lower quadrant. A wound protector or tractor is placed into the incision and twisted to narrow the open aperture.

The air seal system maintains a stable pneumoperitoneum, visibly sustaining the pneumatic working space despite an open circuit. The aperture remains open up to approximately one centimeter squared, with any larger air leak causing a noticeable drop in abdominal distension and loss of working space.
The surgeon adjusts the wound protector retractor to modify the size of the incision opening, ensuring optimal access for the procedure.
The laparoscopic setup allows for the insertion of instruments into a stable pneumoperitoneum, despite the absence of a tight seal around the instruments.

Under direct laparoscopic visualization, a Babcock forceps is inserted through the stoma site to assess the sigmoid loop. The forceps then draws the mobile apex of the sigmoid loop through the abdominal wall for maturation of the loop colostomy.

The operation was performed without complication and illustrates fully the technology's capacity and indicates this operative technique as a potential alternative to single port and trephine alone stoma formation.
